coredata.dc:title: Highly selective, rapid and simple colorimetric detection of Fe3+in fortified foods by L-Cysteine modified AuNP
coredata.dc:creator: Borah, Nirangkush
Kalita, Amlan Jyoti
Guha, Ankur K.
Das, Manash R.
Tamuly, Chandan
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we have developed a simple, stable and cost-effective L-cysteine modified caffeic acid mediated gold nanoparticles (Cys-Caf-AuNP) that showed promising application in detection of Fe3+. Cys-Caf-AuNP was characterized through different advanced analytical techniques which was further implemented in selective detection of Fe3+ in the range of 0.5–5 µM that exhibited LOD (Limit of Detection) of 0.22 µM. Optimization of pH and temperatures were done for conducting efficient detection of Fe3+. Smartphone integrated detection was also approached for detection of Fe3+. Practical reliability of Cys-Caf-AuNP in sensing of Fe3+ was checked carefully in iron fortified foods. The mechanism of selective sensing Fe3+ over Fe2+with Cys-Caf-AuNP was studied through DFT.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cys-Caf-AuNP
Limit of Detection (LOD)
Density Functional Theory (DFT)
Fe3+ detection
Fortified foods

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Collection of Coffea arabica seeds and preparation of extract
Chromatographic analysis of the bioactive compound present in the extract
Smartphone assisted colorimetric detection of Fe3+
Detection of Fe3+ in iron fortified food products
HPLC analysis of the bioactive molecule
Optimization of sensing parameters (Temperature and pH)
Detection of Fe3+ in real samples
Plausible mechanism for sensing of Fe3+ by Cys-Caf-AuNP
DFT insight into the sensing mechanism of Fe3+ by Cys-Caf-AuNP

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank The Director CSIR-North East Institute of Science and Technology for providing valuable advice and help. The authors also thank SEED Division, DST (SEED/TITE/020/ 2017 & DST/SEED/TSP/STI/2020/266), New Delhi for financial assistance to carry out the works. The authors also thank NMPB (Z.180/7/187/CSS/R&D/Aru.01/2018-19-NMPB-IV. A) their support and help.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of Cys-Caf-AuNP (A) UV spectra showing formation (B) XRD Spectra (C) XPS survey spectral analysis (D) High resolution XPS (E) SEM image at dimension 300 nm (F) EDS spectral analysis.
(A) Colorimetric sensing of Fe3+ in the range of 0.5 µM − 5 µM (B) Linear calibration curve depicting change in absorbance upon variation in concentration of Fe3+.
Selectivity study of Cys-Caf-AuNP (A) with different interfering metals (B) with Fe2+ and Fe3+.

Optimized Geometry showing (A) Cys-Caf-AuNP (B) Interaction of Fe (III) with Cys-Caf-AuNP (C) Interaction of Fe (II) with Cys-Caf-AuNP (D) Interaction of Ni (II) with Cys-Caf-AuNP (E) Interaction of Cu (II) with Cys-Caf-AuNP. (F) Interaction of Hg (II) with Cys-Caf-AuNP.
Scheme (a): Formation of Cys-Caf-AuNP. Scheme (b): Interaction of Cys-Caf-AuNP with Fe3+.
Comparison of present study with previously reported data.

row.entry: Oxamic acid-p benzoic acid functionalized AuNP

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Evaluation of Fe3+ in different fortified food samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Recovery (%) = (Found concentration of Fe3+ by proposed method)/ (Added known concentration of Fe3+ in the real samples) × 100.

head.ce:title: Highly selective, rapid and simple colorimetric detection of Fe3+in fortified foods by L-Cysteine modified AuNP

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Natural Product Chemistry Section, CSIR-North East Institute of Science and Technology, Branch Itanagar, Arunachal Pradesh 791110, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR-North East Institute of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we have developed a simple, stable and cost-effective L-cysteine modified caffeic acid mediated gold nanoparticles (Cys-Caf-AuNP) that showed promising application in detection of Fe3+. Cys-Caf-AuNP was characterized through different advanced analytical techniques which was further implemented in selective detection of Fe3+ in the range of 0.5–5 µM that exhibited LOD (Limit of Detection) of 0.22 µM. Optimization of pH and temperatures were done for conducting efficient detection of Fe3+. Smartphone integrated detection was also approached for detection of Fe3+. Practical reliability of Cys-Caf-AuNP in sensing of Fe3+ was checked carefully in iron fortified foods. The mechanism of selective sensing Fe3+ over Fe2+with Cys-Caf-AuNP was studied through DFT.

ce:section.ce:para: The ingenious use of green synthesized nano particles (NPs) has quite an impact on the advanced researches which can be implemented in various practical applications. Execution of these green synthesized nano materials throughout any process has an edge over the expensive and hazardous chemical methods due their safe synthetic route with environment friendly approaches. Green synthesis of NPs may include the use of different plant parts such as flowers, leaves, fruits, stem, roots etc. or involves the use of prokaryotic/eukaryotic cell extracts as precursor to the NPs [1,2]. The presence of bioactive components in plant products like flavonoids, alkaloids or other phenolic compounds are usually responsible for the reduction of the metal ions to their oxidized form. These precursors not only behave like reducing agents but also act as capping agent to synthesize the metallic nanoparticles. Also, the use of plant mediated system results in formation of stable and identical shaped NPs which can be further implemented in mass industrial scale production. Another superiority of green synthesized nanomaterials using plant product lies in the use of aqueous extracts which can be effortlessly prepared. Further the aqueous extracts can be directly utilized in synthesizing the NPs at normal room temperature and pressure extricating the energy and time of any researcher [3–6]. The formation of Au and Ag NPs are quite interesting as they exhibit Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR), a unique property which can be performed in various applications including sensing, bio-labeling etc. Dhas et al. synthesized Sargassum plagiophyllum extract mediated Au NPs of size 65.87 nm. The prepared NPs engendered the inhibition in growth of Escherichia coli and Salmonella typhi depicting the NPs a promising candidate for biomedical applications [7]. Similarly, in a recent report, Ag NPs were prepared using Silybum marinum extract which could be intensively used in different biological and clinical applications including antioxidant, anti-diabetic, anti-aging, anti-inflammatory and cytotoxic properties. Also, the High Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) showed the presence silydianin as the major compounds responsible for reduction and capping of Ag+ to form silver NPs [8]. In one of our recent publications, we described green synthesis of Pd NPs using aqueous extracts of Elsholtzia blanda flower that exhibited intrinsic peroxidase activity. The system could be used as nanozymes that accelerated the oxidation of 3, 3’, 5, 5’-tetramethyl benzidine (TMB) to form a blue color complex. The formation of ox-TMB was inhibited using antioxidants and polyphenols, resulting in detection and evaluation of total antioxidants and total polyphenol contents in different fruit juices and beverages [9].

Iron added or fortified foods are crucial nowadays as these can be taken as dietary supplement for the consumers who suffers from iron deficiencies. In spite of that, the readily ionizable or detachable electrons from iron can produce reactive oxygen (ROS) or nitrogen (RNS) species in human body. These ROS or RNS can further damage in cellular level such as in DNA or proteins. Fe2+ and Fe3+ compounds such as ferrous sulphate (FeSO4), ferric pyrophosphate (Fe4(P2O7)3), ferric sodium EDTA (NaFeEDTA), ferrous fumarate (C4H2FeO4) etc. are fortified into the food products although, the absorption of Fe3+in our body is quite abysmal rather than Fe2+. This leads to further accumulation of iron in the body [10,11]. Hence a simple sensing technique is demanded that can detect Fe3+ selectively over different interferences with a promising applicability towards detection in real samples. Recently, different reports were accounted for detection of Fe3+ on the basis of functionalized gold nano particles. Buduru et al. discussed the formation of oxamic acid and p-amino benzoic acid functionalized gold nano particles (OA-PABA-AuNP) that subsequently detect Fe3+ over the range of 10–400 µM. The LOD value was found to be 5.83 µM. Further the synthesized OA-PABA-AuNP was applied in real sample analysis [12]. Another report suggested the synthesis of fluorescent gold nanoparticles by a growth hormone releasing peptide. The obtained nanoparticle showed high quantum yield of 10.7% and the fluorescence was dynamically quenched with addition of Fe3+ in the range of 2–1000 µM. The sensor showed a low detection limit of 1.4 µM [13]. Fast and selective sensing of Fe3+ was reported by Andreani et al. using ortho-hydroxy benzoic acid capped gold nanoparticles. The method showed a calibration curve of good linearity with a LOD value of 9.19 µM [14]. An interesting study was inspected by Ungor et al. on synthesis of gold nanohybrid using nucleotide adenosine monophosphate. The method was employed in detection of Fe3+ in aqueous samples based on fluorescence quenching upon addition of different concentrations of Fe3+. Different physical parameters were evaluated in the study along with a LOD value of 2.0 µM [15]. On the other hand, visual detection upon addition of different concentration of Fe3+ can be achieved using pyrophosphate functionalized gold nanoparticles with a detection limit of 5.6 µM [16].

The current study mainly described the synthesis of gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) using aqueous extract of Coffea arabica seeds, capped with L-cysteine. The compound responsible for formation of NPs was confirmed by performing chromatographic techniques. The system was found to be superior to other reported literatures as it was cost-effective and easily synthesizable in 1–2 hrs. The synthesized NPs were further characterized through advanced analytical techniques along with its surface morphological study. Moreover, the L-cysteine modified AuNPs (Cys-Caf-AuNP) was implemented for colorimetric detection of Fe3+ selectively up to micromolar level. The color changing upon addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ was also validated by smartphone assisted detection. Selectivity of Fe3+ over other interferences was studied. Further the synthesized NPs were applied in detecting harmful Fe3+ in iron fortified food products.

Chemicals used in synthesis of Cys-Caf-AuNPs along with different analytical assessment involved the use of Gold (III) Chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4, ≥99.9%), L-cysteine (C3H7NO2S, ≥99.0%), Ferric chloride (FeCl3·5H2O, ≥95%), Ferrous chloride (FeCl2, ≥95%), Sodium chloride (NaCl, ≥95%), Calcium chloride (CaCl2, ≥99.5%), Sodium Carbonate (Na2CO3), Copper chloride (CuCl2, ≥94%), Nickel chloride (NiCl2, ≥90%), Lead chloride (PbCl2, ≥90%), Barium chloride (BaCl2, ≥94%), Mercury chloride (HgCl2, ≥99.5%), Stannous chloride (SnCl2, ≥90%), Aluminium chloride (AlCl3, ≥95%) and Chromium chloride (CrCl3·5H2O, ≥95%). All these chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich/Merck (United States) and carefully stored in ambient condition for further use. Solutions were prepared in fresh Milli-Q water of Type-2 grade (pH = 7.1). The glass wares were acquired from Borosil which were carefully washed with both acidic and basic bath and rinsed with acetone. The glass wares were then oven dried at 150 °C for further experiments. For practical application of the synthesized NPs, different iron fortified food products were purchased from local markets.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Collection of Coffea arabica seeds and preparation of extract
ce:section.ce:para: Roasted seed of Coffea arabica were purchased from local grocery stores which subsequently were grinded and macerated. 100 gm of the powdered samples were dissolved in 250 mL of Milli-Q distilled water and boiled for almost 10 min. The solution was then subsequently filtered and the extract (filtrate) collected was stored at 4 °C for further use and analysis. Later the extract was purified using column chromatography and used in synthesis of Cys-Caf-AuNP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chromatographic analysis of the bioactive compound present in the extract
ce:section.ce:para: Water fraction collected after eluting the extract through column was analyzed in HPLC for determination of bioactive compound present. Chromatogram was obtained performing the analysis in Shimadzu Prominence HPLC instrument (LC-20AP) furnished with a Photo Diode Array (PDA) detector. Wavelength range in the detector was set as 200–800 nm. The column used in performing the analysis was composed of C18-Silica gel of 5 µM aperture size. MeOH: H2O = 30:70 was eluted through the column in which 20 µL of the sample was administered via a manual injection. Bioactive compound was identified by the Ultra Violet (UV–Visible) spectra generated from the PDA analysis along with comparing the retention time with the standard.

The synthesis of Cys-Caf-AuNPs was commenced by adding 10 mL of the purified water fraction obtained from the aqueous extract of coffea arabica seeds to 100 mL of boiling 1 mM HAuCl4 solution and stirred at 600 rpm. The color of the solution gradually changed to ruby red color almost in 40–60 min. To that boiling solution 10 mL of 1 mM L-Cysteine was added and allowed to stir for 1 hr. The color of the solution changed from Ruby red to dark purple. The synthesized Cys-Caf-AuNP solution was then kept in 4 °C for further use.

Initially, the origination of Cys-Caf-AuNP colloidal solution was surveilled using Perkin Elmer Lambda 25 UV–Visible spectrophotometer at a wavelength range of 300–900 nm. Optimization conditions along with the selectivity of the NPs were performed using Thermo Scientific 96-well micro-plate reader at the same wavelength range. The XRD (X-Ray Diffraction) pattern was evaluated through Rigaku Ultima IV X-Ray Diffractometer with a scan rate of 4°/min. XPS (X-Ray Photoelectron Spectrophotometer) Spectrum obtained using Thermo-scientific ESCALAB Xi + provided the respective binding energies of the elements present in Cys-Caf-AuNP along with their contents present. The surface morphology was monitored using Semi Electron Microscope (SEM) which was accompanied by Electron Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS). High Resolution-Transmission Electron Microscope (HR-TEM) using a JEOL JEM 2100 with an accelerating voltage of 400 kV was also implemented for surface structure analysis.

For colorimetric detection, 2 mL of the colloidal solution of Cys-Caf-AuNP was diluted 2 times and different concentrations of FeCl3 were added to the solution followed by stirring at 35 °C. The absorbances were measured at 30 min for three times to minimize the handling error. Temperature and PH optimization were parallelly evaluated considering the absorbance maxima at 545 nm. Limit of Detection (LOD) was assessed by calculating the Standard Deviation (SD) and slope (K) from the linear plot of concentration against absorbance at 545 nm followed by the equation as LOD = (3 × SD)/K. Selectivity study of Cys- Caf-AuNP towards Fe3+ was monitored comparing the absorbance change upon addition of different other analytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone assisted colorimetric detection of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Smartphone scanning analysis validated the colorimetric detection of Fe3+. The procedure involved scanning the color change of the solution using a color detector application in mobile. The application provided the intensities of primary colors in percentage upon scanning through the in-built mobile camera. The ratio of blue and red was calculated and a plot was constructed in which blue/red ratio was observed with change in concentration. Digital images of the color change was scanned through 48-megapixel camera and transferred to laptop for further calculations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in iron fortified food products
ce:section.ce:para: Iron fortified foods mainly; iron supplements, fruit juice, bottled water and iron added cornflakes were acquired from the local markets of Arunachal Pradesh, India to check the practical applicability of the synthesized Cys-Caf-AuNP to detect selectively Fe3+. The supplements and the cornflakes were macerated and dissolved in known volume of water, after filtration the filtrates were taken for experimental analysis. Additionally, a known concentration of Fe3+ was spiked to the samples and sonicated for 5 min and vortexed. The spiked samples were then mixed with colloidal solution of Cys-Caf-AuNP. The absorbance spectra were recorded after 30 min to observe the change in SPR peak. Quantitative estimation of Fe3+ present in the samples was achieved applying the calibration curve from the plot of concentration vs absorbance.

All the structures of the compounds and Cys-Caf-AuNP were fully optimized without any symmetry constraint at M06-2X [17] level of theory using TZVP basis set for main group elements and SDD for Fe and Au atoms. Vibrational frequency calculations were executed at the same level of theory to confirm their local minimum nature in the potential energy surface. All local minima structures have all real value of the Hessian matrix. All energies are zero point corrected and calculations were performed using GAUSSIAN16 suite of program [18]. Natural bond orbital (NBO) analyses were performed at the same level of theory to understand the electronic structure of the NPs and its interaction to the Fe3+ [19]. To calculate the amount of charge transfer, extended charge decomposition analysis was implemented using Multiwfn program code [20,21].

ce:section.ce:section-title: HPLC analysis of the bioactive molecule
ce:section.ce:para: HPLC analysis of the water fraction of Coffea arabica accorded the presence of a sharp peak at retention time 3.106 min. The peak was identified as caffeic acid by comparing the retention time of standard caffeic acid along with the UV maximum at 326 nm. Purity index was found to be 0.89 which suggested that the caffeic acid in the water fraction was highly pure than the other compounds present, indicating caffeic acid a major bioactive component in the precursor solution of the NPs. Caffeic acid is considered as a good reducing and capping agent in synthesis of NPs. Along with that, pure caffeic acid and their derivatives shows tremendous anti-diabetic activity. It has the tendency to regulate glucose metabolism by targeting the HEP G2 (Human Liver Cancer cell line) which is insulin resistant [22]. Another report recently depicted the use of caffeic acid and their derivatives as potent inhibitors to SARS-COV-2 [23].These recent researches suggested that caffeic acid incorporated NPs may have a promising affect towards different medicinal and clinical uses. Supplementary Fig. S1 depicted the chromatographic analysis of water fraction of Coffea arabica and standard caffeic acid at gradient elution of 30:70 = MeOH: H2O.

Initially the formation of Cys-Caf-AuNP was confirmed by monitoring the change in the absorbance spectrum in the range of 400–800 nm wavelength. From Fig. 1 . (A), it was observed that unreacted HAuCl4 did not show any SPR spectrum but, with the addition of the pure water fraction containing caffeic acid, characteristic SPR peak was observed at absorbance maximum of 538 nm. This is due to the reduction of Au3+ to Au0 followed by capping. Formation of the Caf-AuNP was in agreement with our previously reported work [24]. Interestingly, with addition of L-cysteine the intensity of SPR peak gradually increased showing a bathochromic shift to absorbance maximum of 545 nm. The red shift was mainly due to the increase in conjugation after addition of L-cysteine that results decreasing resonance energy which subsequently escalated the wavelength. The crystallinity of Cys-Caf-AuNP was confirmed by XRD spectra in Fig. 1. (B). The green synthesized NP showed diffraction peak at 2θ = 38.00°, 44.26°, 64.44° and 77.42° which were ascribed to the planes (111), (200), (220) and (311) respectively. The sharp and strong peaks of Cys-Caf-AuNP clearly implied the higher crystallinity. (111) plane was found to be the predominant orientation suggesting that the NPs were aligned with (111) surface. XPS Survey in Fig. 1. (C) showed the presence of C, O, S and Au elements in Cys-Caf-AyNP. The binding energies of S, C and O were observed in 168.26 eV, 284.82 eV and 532.63 eV (Supplementary Fig. S2) respectively whereas, sharp peak of Au4f at approximately (84–88 eV) sufficiently confirmed presence of Au metal. The high resolution XPS in Fig. 1. (D) depicted the metallic form of Au by exhibiting two spin–orbit splitting peaks at 84.00 eV and 87.63 which were attributed to Au4f7/2 and Au4f5/2 respectively. The XPS spectra were in good agreement with work reported by Cao et al. [25]. Surface morphological property on the other hand was given by analyzing the SEM image in Fig. 1. (E). It was ascertained from the image that the Cys-Caf-AuNP were of spherical in nature with an average diameter of 20–30 nm. SEM was combined with EDS that gave the element compositions in the Cys-Caf-AuNP after wards. The normalized elemental analysis in Fig. 1. (F) has yielded presence of 16.12% of Au, 8.97% of C, 0.49% of O and 0.01% of S by weight. TEM image along with the distribution plot of Cys-Caf-AuNP in Supplementary Fig. S3 also suggested the nanoparticles to have average size of 22.02 ± 1.5 nm.

The stability of the nano-cluster was observed by measured after 15 and 30 days. It was found that the nano-cluster is very stable still 30 days (Supplementary Fig S4). This is one of the additional advantage in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of sensing parameters (Temperature and pH)
ce:section.ce:para:
To get an optimized condition of the experiment for detecting Fe3+ through our proposed Cys-Caf-AuNP system, temperature and pH was taken into consideration. Regular variation in the system upon change in temperature and pH was monitored by recording the UV absorbance maxima at 545 nm. Relative activity (%) in Supplementary Fig. S5 accorded the best and efficient condition for the detection of Fe3+. Relative activity (%) was given by the following equation [9]:
Relative Activity
%
=
AM 1
/
AM 2
× 100
Where, AM1 = Absorbance maxima of Cys-Caf-AuNP at that condition.
AM2 = Highest Absorbance maxima of Cys-Caf-AuNP at a specific condition.

Supplementary Figure S5. (A) suggested that 35 °C was the optimized temperature for detection of Fe3+ whereas, with increase in temperature the activity (%) decreased. This might be because of degradation of capping agent at high temperature resulting in destabilization of Cys-Caf-AuNP. On the other hand, in Supplementary Figure S5. (B), only at neutral pH, the system was found to be efficient rather than implementing it in both acidic and basic condition.

Fe3+ is usually found mainly in different water sources and can be lethal if consumed in high doses. Though requirement of iron is essential in human body, but with high doses it may lead to increase Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) or other radicals that can cause mutations in cellular level or cancer [26]. U.S. Public Health Service declared 0.3 mg/L iron in water to be the maximum limit, excessive may cause different disorders including cellular dysfunction and other critical disorders [12]. To detect the traces of Fe3+, a range of concentration of 0.5 µM − 5 µM was added to the solution of Cys-Caf-AuNP. Fig. 2 . (A) portraited that, with addition of higher concentration of Fe3+ to our system, the SPR peak gradually decreased followed by a little hypsochromic shift (blue shift). Bare Cys-Caf-AuNP was observed in the wavelength of 545 nm but, with addition of Fe3+, the peak got blue shifted to 542 nm. The colloidal solution changed from dark ruby red to colorless upon addition of the analyte. This might be due to the binding interaction of the Cys-Caf-AuNP effectively to Fe3+. The interaction mechanism was described through theoretical evidence in later section. A plot in Fig. 2 (B) was designed for analysis of variation of absorbance upon change in the concentration of Fe3+. The plot subsequently yielded a linear equation of y = -0.13921x + 0.73703 with a standard deviation of 0.010. By applying the standard equation for detection of LOD, the LOD value was found to be 0.22 µM. Table 1 , presented a comparison of our obtained data with previously reported studies.

Since the color change was visualized by naked eye, a smartphone integrated detection of Fe3+ was approached for easy analysis. Upon scanning the color of the solution using smartphone, it was observed that Blue/Red (B/R) ratio decreases gradually with addition of higher concentration of Fe3+. The regression equation was originated as y = -0.046x + 1.22 with a R-square value of 0.987 that specified good correlation of all the data in the linear plot. Rationally, this smartphone scanning detection was used in practical applicability by scanning the change in color of the solution after adding the sample to colloidal solution of Cys-Caf-AuNP. By calculating the obtained B/R ratio, corresponding concentration can be evaluated. Supplementary Fig. S6 (A) clearly illustrated the color scanning procedure with the help of a smartphone where as, supplementary Fig. S6 (B) depicted the change in B/R ratio upon change in concentration.

Highly selective response of Cys-Caf-AuNP towards Fe3+ was substantiated through Fig. 3 .(A) and Fig. 3.(B). The Fig. 3.(A) depicted the change in UV absorbance spectrum after addition of different metal ions as interferences. It was observed that no such considerable changes were detected upon addition of same concentration of Na+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Hg2+ and Sn2+ respectively (color change from left to right) whereas, in presence of Fe3+ the dark ruby red color became colorless suggesting the selective interaction of Cys-Caf-AuNP with Fe3+. Furthermore, interesting results were noticed in Fig. 3. (B), when Cys-Caf-AuNP was incorporated with Fe2+. Change in the oxidation state of iron to + 2 state also could not interfere with the proposed system as there was little change in the SPR peak rather than Fe3+. Supplementary Fig. S7 showed the change in UV absorbance on addition of other trivalent metal atoms such that Al3+ and Cr3+ with Cys-Caf-AuNP in comparison with Fe3+. No such changes were noticed upon addition of these trivalent metals whereas addition of Fe3+ significantly changed the absorbance maxima.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed system Cys-Caf-AuNP could be reliably used in detection of Fe3+. Four different concentrations of 1 µM, 2 µM, 3 µM and 4 µM of FeCl3 were spiked to the water samples collected. The samples were than incorporated with Cys-Caf-AuNP and their UV spectra were recorded (Supplementary Fig. S8). The absorbance maxima of the corresponding spectra were determined and plotted in the linear calibration curve in Fig. 2. (B) to obtain the concentration of Fe3+ present in the sample. Table 2 clearly evidenced the found Fe3+ in the samples with a high recovery percentage suggesting presence of extra Fe3+ other than the spiked value. It was found that the mixed fruit juice contains a higher amount of Fe3+ than in the other real samples. These may be due to the fortification of the food products with Fe3+ conjugated compounds to enhance their mineral supplements. Whereas, iron supplements yielded lower recovery rates indicating non availability of Fe3+ or formation of some other complexes with the added concentration of Fe3+. Furthermore, the acquired results were validated using titrimetric method [27] by forming EDTA complex. The results were also shown in Table 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plausible mechanism for sensing of Fe3+ by Cys-Caf-AuNP
ce:section.ce:para: The plausible mechanism (Scheme 1) was established by correlating different data and observing other similar work which were further supported by theoretical analysis. Scheme (a) pictured the formation of Cys-Caf-AuNP, where 2-S-cysteinyl-caffeic acid was responsible for reducing and capping of Au3+. 2-S-cysteinyl-caffeic acid was formed due to the oxidation of caffeic acid upon addition of L-cysteine [28]. Further, when the Cys-Caf-AuNP interacted with Fe3+, the 2-S-cysteinyl-caffeic acid moiety formed chelation with Fe3+, that subsequently led to migration of electron density towards the chelating site leaving the Au (0) destabilized. This results in degradation of Cys-Caf-AuNP with increase in addition of Fe3+. The interaction could easily be represented by Scheme (b). Moreover, it was found that bare caffeic acid stabilized gold nanoparticles could not able to bind or interact with Fe3+ and hence modification of the bare nanoparticles with the L-cysteine was necessary (Supplementary Fig. S9).

ce:section.ce:section-title: DFT insight into the sensing mechanism of Fe3+ by Cys-Caf-AuNP
ce:section.ce:para: Computational calculations further assisted the plausible mechanism. The formation and stability of Cys-Caf-AuNP was confirmed through Fig. 4 . (A). The optimized geometry depicted that the Au-O bond distances were found to be 2.894 and 3.068 Å with a binding energy of 7.8 kcal/mol. Optimized geometry of Cys-Caf-AuNP upon interaction with Fe3+ in Fig. 4. (B) showed that, Fe (III)-O bond distances were 2.151 and 2.434 Å having binding energy of 19.1 kcal/mol. The changes in the HOMO of Cys-Caf-AuNP (Supplementary Fig. S10) suggested the contribution of electron density from the nearest O atom of OH to bind with Fe3+. Interestingly, the complexation of Fe (II) centre with Cys-Caf-AuNP was not that stable as revealed by weaker binding energy of only 5.1 kcal/mol. The weaker binding of Fe (II) over Fe (III) might be due to the more electrostatic interaction between the negatively charged oxygen atoms with Fe (III) centre rather than Fe (II) centre. The calculated amount of charge transfer from O atoms to Fe (III) centre as revealed by extended charge decomposition analysis is 0.92 whereas it is only 0.21 with Fe (II) centre. The optimized geometry of Cys-Caf-AuNP with Fe (II) centre is shown in Fig. 4. (C). The calculated Fe (II)-O bond lengths were also longer (3.354 and 3.077 Å) which also suggested weaker binding. Similar weaker binding was also observed for Ni(II), Cu(II) and Hg(II) complexes. The binding energy for Ni(II) complex was 4.3 kcal/mol while it was 3.8 kcal/mol for Cu(II) complex and 3.1 kcal/mol for Hg(II) complex. This indicated that binding of Fe(III) was more feasible rather than Ni(II), Cu(II) and Hg(II) complexes [Fig. 4(D), Fig. 4(E) and Fig. 4(F)]. The weaker binding of Fe2+, Ni2+, Cu2+ and Hg2+ over Fe3+ might be due to the more electrostatic interaction between the negatively charged oxygen atoms with Fe3+ centre rather than Fe2+/Ni2+/Cu2+/Hg2+ centre. Further, the DFT study was carried for other metal ion such as Ba2+, Ca2+, Na+ etc. But the cluster NPs was very unstable and very labile. So, the optimized structure was not observed. Moreover, the trivalent metal ion such as Al3+ and Cr3+ ion shown very labile cluster. Similarly, the optimized structure was not observed for trivalent metal ion also.

This study offered a comprehensive idea for highly selective utilization of Cys-Caf-AuNP in detection of Fe3+. Synthesis of the Cys-Caf-AuNPs using Coffea arabica extract showed the greenery of the process rather using different toxic materials for synthesis. Cys-Caf-AuNP also exhibit high selectivity and sensitivity towards Fe3+ which led to one of its major applications in detecting Fe3+ in different real samples. We also tried to understand the mechanism of sensing using theoretical and computational techniques i.e., DFT analysis. Further studies on the use of Cys-Caf-AuNP towards detection and other industrial aspects should give a way through for prolific future prospects.

Nirangkush Borah: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Resources, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Amlan Jyoti Kalita: Validation, Software. Ankur K. Guha: Validation, Methodology. Manash R. Das: Characterization, Validation. Chandan Tamuly: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank The Director CSIR-North East Institute of Science and Technology for providing valuable advice and help. The authors also thank SEED Division, DST (SEED/TITE/020/ 2017 & DST/SEED/TSP/STI/2020/266), New Delhi for financial assistance to carry out the works. The authors also thank NMPB (Z.180/7/187/CSS/R&D/Aru.01/2018-19-NMPB-IV. A) their support and help.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107480.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
